per_question.predicted_answer:
Based on the provided document chunks, gross margin is not a useful metric for American Express, and therefore its change is not discussed.

The reason is that, as a financial services company, American Express does not report a traditional gross margin (Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold). Instead, its comparable top-line metric is presented as "Total revenues net of interest expense" [PAGE 96 | CHUNK 38bad7b4-694e-4f22-a5e4-fc7293ae617b]. Key costs directly related to revenue generation do not appear as a single cost of goods sold line but as separate components like provisions for credit losses and Card Member rewards expenses [PAGE 96 | CHUNK 38bad7b4-694e-4f22-a5e4-fc7293ae617b].

The discussion focuses on changes in "Total revenues net of interest expense," which increased by 25% ($10.5 billion) in FY2022 compared to FY2021 [PAGE 47 | CHUNK 14ddf541-16f9-4207-9d6d-919113a0e5cd]. This increase was primarily driven by:
- A $6.2 billion (25%) increase in Discount revenue, driven by higher billed business.
- A $2.1 billion (28%) increase in Net interest income, driven by higher loan balances and interest rates.
- A $1.2 billion (36%) increase in Service fees and other revenue, driven by foreign exchange and travel-related fees [PAGE 47 | CHUNK 14ddf541-16f9-4207-9d6d-919113a0e5cd].
